turns.content: I'm back in the game after five years away from Rust and I'm trying to understand how a no_std bootloader actually loads an application into RAM. Specifically, if I have my linker script defining memory regions for flash and SRAM, how does the boot process ensure the application code is placed at exactly the offset the CPU expects?

The core concept you're missing is that a bootloader isn't just Rust code sitting in memory; it's an assembly or C program that sets up the hardware state before handing control to your Rust application. You need to think of this as a two-stage boot process where the first stage is the bootloader itself, which runs from flash and performs initialization like setting up the stack pointer and clearing global variables used by the no_std environment. The bootloader then jumps to the address defined in your linker script for the main application binary. So when you're writing your Rust crate with cfg!(feature = "bootloader") enabled, you're actually building a separate binary that gets linked into the same ELF file as the application but loaded at a different memory address by the bootloader's jump instruction. Does this distinction between the bootloader binary and the application binary make sense to you?

I think I see where my mistake is coming from, but I was assuming the Rust compiler would handle all the memory layout automatically like in std environments. I don't understand how the linker script actually maps out the flash regions for both the bootloader and the application without causing a collision or overwriting critical data. Can you explain more concretely what goes wrong if my linker script doesn't properly separate these regions?

The common mistake is forgetting that in no_std environments, there's no automatic memory management or garbage collection; everything must be explicitly defined in your linker script using the .section directive to carve out specific areas for code and data. If you don't define a distinct section for the bootloader binary, the linker will place it right next to your application code, causing them to overwrite each other when loaded into flash. For instance, if your application expects its code at 0x1000 but the bootloader occupies that space or extends beyond it, the CPU will execute garbage data instead of valid instructions. You need to ensure your linker script explicitly reserves a region for the bootloader section before defining your application's sections so they never overlap in memory. What specific error message are you seeing when you try to build this combined binary?

I'm getting a relocation error saying that symbols from the bootloader are being referenced at wrong addresses, which suggests my linker script isn't handling the jump properly. I tried using .section with the bootloader name but didn't account for how the CPU actually jumps between these regions during boot. How do I ensure the jump is valid and doesn't cause a crash when transferring control?

The relocation error happens because the linker assumes everything starts at offset zero, but in your case, you're trying to load two binaries into the same file with different entry points and memory layouts. You need to explicitly define separate sections for flash and data in your linker script, ensuring the bootloader section ends before the application section begins. Then, when you link both the Rust crate (compiled with no_std features) and the assembly bootloader into a single ELF, the loader will correctly place them at their respective offsets. The key is that the jump instruction in the bootloader must point to the exact address where your Rust binary starts after compilation, which you calculate based on your linker script's output. Have you tried using a linker script file with explicit memory map definitions for flash and SRAM?

I have a linker script but I'm not sure how to properly align my sections so the application loads at the correct offset without corrupting the bootloader or leaving uninitialized code in memory. Is there a specific way to handle the alignment of the Rust crate's output within the no_std context when combined with an assembly bootloader?

When compiling your Rust crate for embedded use, you must explicitly set the linker flags to ensure proper alignment and placement relative to your bootloader sections. You should compile your application binary with a specific entry point address that matches what your bootloader expects it to jump to, which is defined in your linker script's memory map. The Rust compiler output will be placed at the offset specified by .section .text { ALIGN(4) } directives in your linker script, ensuring that when the bootloader jumps, it lands exactly on valid code rather than padding or garbage data. You also need to ensure that your no_std crate doesn't assume any global variables exist before initialization, so the linker script must reserve enough space for those variables after the application is loaded but before the main function runs. What specific alignment requirements does your hardware architecture impose?
